heat_safety.title: Heat Safety
heat_safety.overview: Makkah temperatures during Hajj can exceed 45°C (113°F). Heat-related illness is a leading cause of Hajj hospitalizations. Protect yourself proactively.

hydration.rule: Drink at least 250ml (1 cup) of water every 15–20 minutes while outdoors — even if you don't feel thirsty.
tips: Carry a spray bottle with water — misting skin reduces body temperature
Drink electrolyte solutions or oral rehydration salts (ORS) to replace lost salts
Avoid sugary drinks and caffeine — they increase dehydration
Zamzam water is available throughout Masjid al-Haram — use it freely

prevention: Wear a white umbrella or use a hat — direct sun exposure dramatically increases heat risk
Wear lightweight, breathable white or light-colored clothing over Ihram in hot conditions
Stay in shaded or air-conditioned areas during midday (10 AM – 4 PM) whenever possible
Use cooling towels or pour cool water on your head and neck
Wear sandals that protect feet from hot pavement — burns are common
Rest frequently — do not push yourself in extreme heat

heat_exhaustion.title: Heat Exhaustion — Warning Signs
heat_exhaustion.action: Move to a cool shaded area immediately. Remove excess clothing. Drink cool water slowly. Apply cool wet cloths to skin. If not improving within 30 minutes, seek medical help.

heatstroke.title: Heatstroke — Medical Emergency
symptoms: Hot, red, dry or damp skin
heatstroke.action: CALL 997 (Ambulance) IMMEDIATELY. This is a life-threatening emergency. While waiting: move to cool area, remove clothing, apply ice packs to neck/armpits/groin, fan the person, do NOT give water to an unconscious person.

crowd_safety.title: Crowd Safety
crowd_safety.overview: Hajj is the world's largest annual gathering with up to 3 million pilgrims. Understanding crowd dynamics can save your life.
rules: Never go against the flow of crowd movement — always move in the same direction as the crowd
If caught in a crowd surge: turn sideways, protect your chest with crossed arms, move diagonally to the edge
Stay with your group — designate a meeting point in case of separation
Wear your Hajj ID wristband and keep emergency contact information on your person
Move during off-peak hours (after midnight, early morning) for rituals like Rami if your health permits
If you fall: curl into a ball protecting your head, and call for help loudly
Do not stop to pick up items in dense crowds
Keep children and elderly close — use physical contact (holding hands/arm)
crowd_safety.emergency_exits: Learn the emergency exits of your tent camp and the Jamarat bridge before you need them.
crowd_safety.communication: Saudi authorities use colored wristbands and designated nationality zones — follow your group leader's instructions always.

health_requirements.title: Health Requirements & Vaccinations

mandatory_vaccinations.name: Meningococcal Meningitis (ACYW135)
mandatory_vaccinations.timing: Must be received at least 10 days before arrival in Saudi Arabia
mandatory_vaccinations.notes: Required for visa — ensure your vaccination certificate is signed and stamped

mandatory_vaccinations.name: COVID-19 Vaccine
mandatory_vaccinations.requirement: Per current Saudi Ministry requirements — check Nusuk platform for updates
mandatory_vaccinations.notes: Requirements may change — always verify before departure

recommended_vaccinations.name: Influenza (Flu)
recommended_vaccinations.notes: Highly recommended due to close contact with pilgrims worldwide

recommended_vaccinations.name: Pneumococcal
recommended_vaccinations.notes: Recommended for elderly and those with chronic conditions

health_requirements.chronic_conditions: If you have diabetes, heart disease, asthma, or other chronic conditions: consult your physician before Hajj. Carry sufficient medication (extra supply recommended). Carry a medical summary in Arabic if possible.

lost_pilgrim.title: Lost Pilgrim Protocol
if_you_are_lost: Stay calm — Saudi authorities have robust systems to reunite pilgrims
Do not wander — stay in one place if possible
Look for Saudi police officers (white uniforms) or Hajj mission staff
Go to the nearest Hajj Mission office — each country has one in Makkah
Call your group leader's number (save it before leaving)
Show your Hajj wristband — it contains your nationality and group information
Go to Masjid al-Haram if in Makkah — Hajj authorities maintain coordination centers there
if_someone_is_missing: Contact your country's Hajj Mission office
File a report with Saudi police at the nearest station if needed
Provide: full name, nationality, passport number, photograph, last known location and time
prevention: Memorize your tent camp number and zone
Save your group leader's number OFFLINE
Write your hotel and camp address on a card — keep it on your person
Take a photo of your group at the start of each major ritual
Agree on a landmark meeting point before each crowd event
Elderly and disabled pilgrims should always have a companion

general_tips.title: Footwear
general_tips.body: Wear comfortable, well-fitted sandals. Ground temperatures can exceed 70°C and cause severe burns to bare feet. Never walk barefoot on tarmac.

general_tips.title: Phone Charging
general_tips.body: Carry a portable power bank. Staying charged is essential for navigation, emergency calls, and group coordination.

general_tips.title: Respiratory Hygiene
general_tips.body: Wear a mask in crowded areas if you have a respiratory condition. Respiratory infections spread easily in large gatherings.

general_tips.title: Overexertion
general_tips.body: Hajj is physically demanding. Rest when needed — pushing through illness is dangerous. Missing a non-obligatory act is better than collapsing.

general_tips.title: Food Safety
general_tips.body: Eat freshly prepared food from established vendors. Avoid foods left in heat. Traveler's diarrhea is common — stay hydrated and carry ORS.

general_tips.title: Sleep
general_tips.body: Aim for 6+ hours of sleep each night despite the spiritual energy. Fatigue causes accidents and impairs judgment in crowds.
